prompt: I think Shakespeare is massively overrated. His plays have nothing to teach us today. Tell me what you actually think about that claim.
response:
I think the claim is half-right and half-wrong—exactly the kind of verdict that would have made Shakespeare himself smile.

1. Over-rated?  
   If “over-rated” means “mentioned more often than he is actually read,” then yes: the brand-name “Shakespeare” is sometimes used as a cultural password rather than a living playwright. But that is a social phenomenon, not a literary judgment. Once you clear away the tourist-shop fridge-magnet Bardolatry and sit down with the texts, the work is still doing things few writers manage: compressing an entire moral universe into a single line, letting characters argue with themselves in real time, showing how language itself can be bent until it reveals new thoughts. Those feats don’t fade just because syllabus designers and advertisers keep photocopying the same three soliloquies.

2. Nothing to teach us today?  
   That part is simply unsustainable if you test it against the plays rather than the clichés. A few snapshots:

   – Coriolanus: a populist strong-man who despises the very masses he manipulates. The play asks how far a society should go in “giving the people what they want,” and how quickly adoration can flip to rage. Twitter mobs, cable news, 2024 election cycles—every beat is there.  
   – Measure for Measure: a politician cracks down on sexual licence while secretly coercing a novice nun. The plot is #MeToo centuries before the hashtag.  
   – Othello: workplace racism, toxic male insecurity, and the lethal speed of online-style misinformation—only the “internet” is Iago’s whisper campaign.  
   – King Lear: a retirement-and-dementia story that tracks the moment when a parent’s authority dissolves and the kids discover the estate is entangled with elder care, inheritance law, and climate disaster (the storm on the heath is the 17th-century version of extreme weather).  
   – The histories: a cycle about how fragile nation-states are when legitimacy is performative and every king needs a spin doctor. Try watching Henry IV parts 1 & 2 the week a government collapses and tell me it feels antique.

3. The real reason people say “nothing to teach us”  
   Usually they mean: “I can’t see myself in the costume drama verse.” Fair enough—iambic pentameter and codpieces are alienating if nobody shows you the door in. But once you hear the characters speaking your own dialect of anxiety, the clothes fall away. The gate-keeping problem is educational, not textual. A good staging (or even a smart film adaptation) can make a 1590s line land like a push-notification.

4. The deeper, sneaky value  
   Shakespeare’s age believed less than we do in psychological interiority; it had no DSM, no therapy culture. So the plays externalise inner states—jealousy as a handkerchief, grief as a ghost, ambition as a dagger. That very strangeness is useful: it gives us a set of metaphors to inspect feelings we normally label with pop-psych clichés. In short, the plays are a foreign country that turns out to speak our dialect of distress once we learn to listen.

So my blunt verdict: if you come away thinking the plays have “nothing to say,” it’s probably because the surrounding culture has buried them under souvenir snow-globes. Crack the snow-globe, actually read or watch the work, and the overrated corpse keeps twitching back to life—annoyingly, irresistibly, and usually with a better insult for the occasion than any of us can muster.
